data.title: RYLD: When Beta Fails, Income Delivers
data.description:
Since I issued a Buy on the Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF (RYLD) in July last year, the call has gone both right and wrong. In total return terms, RYLD has returned ~11% but has lagged the underlying Russell 2000 significantly (rallied ~21%). I had envisioned a flat market for the Russell 2000, where RYLD's aggressive option positioning reaps the maximum reward over the underlying; that has not happened with small caps rallying strongly (through volatility).

RYLD's option coverage is quite aggressive, and small-cap index options usually trade with greater premiums (because of greater underlying volatility). This makes RYLD a powerful option income strategy by virtue of its positioning. So, even if it captures partial upside when small caps rally, aggressive income in markets that are flat to slightly down makes up for the opportunity losses. Investors should only brace for a scenario where small caps undergo a deep correction: this is a zone where conviction in long-term rebounds is required. RYLD's yield is just about double digits (~12% on a TTM basis), somewhat sustainable through a combination of capital appreciation (although capped) and option income in the long term. So NAV erosion is not a systemic problem here, and rebounds can be capitalized on as long as the direction stays upward bound.

Over the next few quarters, I expect the small-cap space to remain range-bound (or slightly under pressure) as the odds of lower growth and a stickier inflation scenario are high. In both scenarios, RYLD's aggressive option income and drawdown mitigation (limited but strong) should be able to actually show alpha over the Russell 2000 (something I expected last July). Hence, RYLD continues to be a Buy. The large downside scenario cannot be ruled out, but despite the small-cap outperformance over the last 9 months, valuations are at historical lows compared to the large-cap space. That leaves room for mean reversion once the macro visibility improves.

Portfolio Structure

RYLD has a straightforward methodology. It aggressively writes options on the full underlying Russell 2000 portfolio (long Global X Russell 2000 ETF). I checked this by downloading the full holdings from the ETF website and found 5,219 short calls on the Russell 2000 index. The notional value of this exposure is ~$1.3 billion (5,219 contracts x 100 multiplier x 2,465 strike), matching the fund's AUM. The choice of strikes is important, but this does not look like an active call because RYLD tracks the CBOE Russell 2000 BuyWrite Index, and BuyWrite indices generally write options ATM. Barring slight interpretation of ATM strike (rounding) discrepancies, I do not see a lot of active calls going into the strategy.

That means high premiums due to both the ATM positioning as well as elevated premiums anyway. For context, you can look at the ATM option premiums for the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) and SPY. I checked them as of April 17th, 2026, and for SPY, the ATM strike of $702 is trading at ~$14 for the May 22nd, 2026 expiry. For IWM, the $270 strike for the same expiry is trading at ~$7.7. That is ~2% income for SPY ATM options and ~2.9% for IWM ATM options. The exact differences will differ depending on how volatile markets become (gaps are likely to only increase).

The Downside Argument

Despite the small-cap outperformance over the past 9 months, they are still trading at a deep relative discount to larger caps. Most of the relative apathy has been a result of relative strength in large caps rather than historical weakness in small caps. That has started to change. Since September-October last year, the large-cap space, dominated by tech and AI themes by market cap, has started cooling. That has seen a lot of rotation into international and value markets. Some of the rotation has spilled over to the broader markets as well. As the tech-AI play continues to await a fresh round of catalysts (i.e., confirmation of cyclical strength in semis and equipment, CapEx digestion and monetization in hyperscalers, and pricing power confirmation in SaaS), the relative rotation into small caps should also keep sharp downsides at bay over the next 2-3 quarters at least.

We have discussed this in my earlier thesis too, but here is fresh evidence of what happens if sharp drawdowns do open up. During the peak of the Middle East crisis, when IWM was down by over 8%, RYLD was down by 2% less and theoretically could have earned back that ~6% drawdown too if markets had decided to stay at the lows (through 1-2% option income over the next few months). The drawdown mitigation is limited, but RYLD's ATM strike positioning, 100% portfolio coverage, and higher option premiums, especially in volatile environments, make it a relatively better covered call writing vehicle when it comes to the magnitude of drawdown mitigation. However, sharp downsides still remain a zone for worry for investors — a zone where the strategy becomes less market-agnostic and relies on a long-term rebound eventually. I do not see odds of a very sharp downside ahead: any drawdown of up to 10% can be relatively absorbed over a few months, the total return cushioned by the income if not distributed.

If I Get It Wrong

That is what happened since July last year. And this is a happy scenario to be in. Investors claim a lot less of the rally, but they see NAVs steady and payouts still funded by underlying capital appreciation — opportunity loss but income and NAV both intact.

The Base Scenario

The base scenario is, of course, where I see the markets headed. Low growth and sticky inflation could mean small-cap stocks face higher rate sensitivity (due to more floating rate and short-duration debt that needs refinancing). In a lower growth environment, the relative lack of brand strength often means weaker pricing power and an inability to pass on higher input costs, pressuring margins. If the funding environment pulls back due to higher credit risks, that too increases funding pressures on small caps. The combined weights in energy and materials (relative winners in a stagflation scenario) are just about double digits, so the support is limited if a stagflation-like scenario emerges.

On the contrary, a flat to slightly pressured environment for small caps often comes with higher volatility, which means that a tougher time for the underlying translates to a reasonably strong income generation regime for RYLD. And this is what happens when the small-cap space ends flat (of course through rallies and corrections, i.e., higher volatility). In theory, RYLD should easily add 1-2% a month if the Russell 2000 does not rally from here. In practice, higher realized volatility than implied could mean less option premium capture over time. Still, RYLD could add 5-10% (depending on volatility and regime specifics) over a year - the flat conditions from October to March (through extreme volatility, of course) saw ~2.5% gains in RYLD in total return terms.

Overall, RYLD retains a Buy because the option layer is aggressively poised to capture gains from a flat market end-to-end and through possible volatility. The only scenario investors need to watch for is a sharp drawdown, which, though unlikely, could mean a more long-term wait for the strategy to pull back.
